question: [File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user supply a crafted Base32-encoded alias via the GraphQL account query to retrieve an entity whose `key` field encodes a fee-schedule key, thereby learning the exact signing material needed to submit a TokenFeeScheduleUpdate transaction that modifies custom fees outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user craft a 20-byte EVM address whose first 4 bytes and next 8 bytes are all zero (the long-form entity-ID path in EntityServiceImpl.getByEvmAddressAndType) to resolve an arbitrary entity by numeric ID, bypassing the evm_address column lookup and exposing that entity's `balance` and `autoRenewAccountId` fields used in fee calculations?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user enumerate all active entity aliases via repeated GraphQL account queries using the `findByAlias` path to discover which accounts hold fee-collector roles (collector_account_id in custom_fee table), enabling targeted fee-manipulation attacks against those accounts?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user send a hex-encoded EVM address with the `0x` prefix stripped to bypass the regex pattern `^(0x)?[a-fA-F0-9]{40}$` validation and cause `decodeEvmAddress` to produce an unexpected byte array that resolves to a different entity than intended, returning that entity's fee-related fields (`balance`, `key`) to the attacker?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the SQL condition `deleted is not true` (which returns TRUE for NULL values) in the `findByAlias` native query to retrieve entities where `deleted IS NULL`, including entities in transitional states whose `autoRenewAccountId` or `balance` fields reflect stale fee-payment state, leading to incorrect fee-payer resolution?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the SQL condition `deleted is not true` in `findByEvmAddress` to retrieve entities with `deleted IS NULL` (not yet marked deleted), exposing their `key` bytes which may contain a fee-schedule key that the attacker can use to craft a fee-modification transaction outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a malformed Base32 string (e.g., containing padding characters stripped by the `^[A-Z2-7]+$` regex but accepted by Apache Commons Base32.decode) to cause `decodeBase32` to produce a byte array that matches a different entity's alias in the database, returning that entity's `balance` and fee-related fields?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user issue thousands of concurrent `findByEvmAddress` GraphQL queries (each within the 200-complexity and 10-depth limits) to exhaust the HikariCP database connection pool, causing fee-processing transactions in the importer to time out and be recorded with incorrect `chargedTxFee` values?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user issue thousands of concurrent `findByAlias` GraphQL queries to exhaust database connections (as evidenced by the GraphQLHighDBConnections alert threshold of 75%), causing fee-schedule update transactions processed by the importer to fail or be delayed, effectively freezing custom fee state at stale values?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user use the `findByEvmAddress` path to retrieve an entity's `stakedAccountId` and `stakePeriodStart` fields, then use that information to predict when staking rewards (which affect effective account balance used for fee payment) will be distributed, and time a fee-modification transaction to exploit a low-balance window?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user query the `balance` field of a fee-collector account via `findByAlias` to determine whether that collector has insufficient balance to pay network fees, then submit a transaction that triggers fee collection at a moment when the collector cannot pay, causing the fee collection to fail and effectively zeroing out custom fees?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `convertCurrency` integer division in `GraphQlUtils` (e.g., `tinybars / 100_000_000L` for HBAR) to obtain a truncated balance value that misrepresents a fee-collector account's true balance, leading to incorrect fee-sufficiency assessments by downstream consumers of the GraphQL API?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user query the `autoRenewAccount` nested field (which triggers a second `findById` call via `EntityRepository`) for a fee-collector entity to discover the auto-renew payer's balance and key, enabling the attacker to assess whether the auto-renew fee path can be disrupted to cause entity expiration and removal of associated custom fee schedules?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user craft a GraphQL query that requests `autoRenewAccount { autoRenewAccount { autoRenewAccount { ... } } }` up to the depth limit of 10 to trigger 10 chained `EntityRepository.findById` calls per request, exhausting DB connections and delaying fee-schedule ingestion in the importer?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user use the `findByAlias` query to retrieve the `key` field (raw protobuf bytes) of a token treasury account, then decode those bytes offline to reconstruct the public key and use it to identify the signing key required to authorize a fee-schedule update transaction?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a zero-padded EVM address (12 zero bytes followed by 8 bytes encoding a target entity ID) to the `findByEvmAddress` path, causing `EntityServiceImpl` to call `findById` instead of `findByEvmAddress`, and thereby retrieve the `balance` and `key` of any entity by numeric ID without knowing its EVM address?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `findByAlias` returns the full entity row (via `select *`) including sensitive fields like `public_key`, `key`, and `balance` to enumerate fee-collector accounts and their signing keys, enabling construction of unauthorized fee-modification transactions?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user use the `findByEvmAddress` native query (which returns `select *` from entity) to retrieve the `receiver_sig_required` field of a fee-collector account, and use that information to determine whether a fee-payment transaction to that collector requires an additional signature, enabling the attacker to craft transactions that bypass fee collection?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user send a GraphQL query with `evmAddress` set to `0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000000` (all zeros) to trigger the `findById(0)` path in `EntityServiceImpl`, potentially returning a system entity (entity ID 0) whose fields include fee-schedule-related keys?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `OnValidationErrorStrategy.RETURN_NULL` behavior in the GraphQL validation wiring to submit an `evmAddress` that fails the regex pattern but is still processed by `decodeEvmAddress`, causing an unexpected byte array to be passed to `findByEvmAddress` and potentially matching an unintended entity?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user use the `pendingReward` field returned via the `findByAlias` entity lookup to determine the exact staking reward amount pending for a fee-collector account, and time a fee-modification transaction to occur before the reward is distributed, reducing the collector's effective balance below the fee threshold?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user use the `expirationTimestamp` field returned by `findByEvmAddress` to determine when a fee-collector entity will expire, and submit transactions timed to occur after expiration (when the entity is deleted and `deleted is not true` would exclude it), causing fee collection to fail silently?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user issue a high volume of `findByAlias` queries (each under the 200-complexity limit) to trigger the `GraphQLQueryLatency > 1s` alert threshold, causing the database to slow down and fee-schedule update transactions to be processed out of order, resulting in stale fee schedules being applied?
